figures.caption: Fig. 1: System deployed for hardware experiments. Each quadrupedal robot is provided with a mount ending with a spherical joint connected to a bar of 2 m length. The task-relevant frames are highlighted.

TABLE I: Success rate (SR), execution frequency ( f ), and average path lengths for succesful trials of agent1, agent2, and the object (mean ± std, in m) across experiments. The optimization-based planner is evaluated with local map knowledge for different nsamples and with full map knowledge using nsamples = 1500.

Fig. 4: Trajectory comparison in the Boxes scenario. From left to right: MAPPO, Optimization-based (local map, nsamples = 1500), Ours, and Optimization-based (full map, nsamples = 1500). The black line denotes the object path, colored lines represent the agents. Red stars indicate intermediate waypoints.

Fig. 5: Waypoints-following on empty terrain experiment. Top: system configuration at the start and after reaching two predefined waypoints (red stars). Bottom: real-time exteroceptive observations, overlaid with the trajectories of the agents’ bases and the position of the world frame.

Fig. 6: Waypoint-following on cluttered terrain experiment. Top: system configuration at the start and after reaching two predefined waypoints (red stars). Bottom: real-time exteroceptive observations highlighting obstacle locations, overlaid with the trajectories of the agents’ bases and the position of the world frame.

Fig. 2: Policy architecture. Single-robot feasible paths are sampled from the terrain, and the direction of the next waypoint defines the high-level command hl ob jc. Together with system-level observations, hl ob jo, these are provided to the high-level policy, which outputs highlevel actions hl ob ja. These represent the rotated low-level commands lli basei c, which are executed by the trained locomotion policies.
TABLE II: Summary of all parameters introduced in Sec. III.
